planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after the last frost in a location with partial shade; mulch the root zone to retain moisture and moderate soil temperature.

directions: Carolina Snailseed is a twining woody vine native to woodland edges, thickets, and fencerows across the southeastern United States. It produces small, heart-shaped to kidney-shaped leaves and inconspicuous greenish flowers on female plants in summer, followed by dense clusters of brilliant red berries in fall that persist into winter and provide important food for mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and hermit thrushes. Grow in average to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; it tolerates a range of soil conditions once established. Note that the berries, while highly attractive to wildlife, are toxic to humans — plant where children will not have unsupervised access to the fruit.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Divide established rhizome clumps in early spring before new growth emerges and replant at the same depth in moist, humus-rich soil; keep consistently moist through the first growing season.

directions: Moonseed is a deciduous, twining native vine of moist woodland edges, stream banks, and thicket margins throughout eastern North America, valued for its bold, broadly shield-shaped leaves and striking blue-black berries in fall. The crescent-shaped seed inside each berry inspired the common name and distinguishes it from the true wild grapes it resembles — the berries are highly toxic and must never be eaten. It spreads readily by underground rhizomes to form dense, colonizing patches, making it ideal for naturalizing moist, shaded slopes and stabilizing stream banks. Grow in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade; it tolerates periodic flooding and deep shade better than most native vines.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Transplant in spring into dry, well-drained, rocky or sandy soil in a location with afternoon shade; avoid overwatering, as this desert species is highly susceptible to root rot in heavy or poorly drained soils.

directions: Watson's Dutchman's Pipe is a slender, drought-adapted native perennial vine of desert washes, rocky canyon slopes, and scrubby edges in southern Arizona, New Mexico, and adjacent Mexico. It produces narrow, elongated leaves and distinctive, S-curved, brownish-purple pipe-shaped flowers that serve as an exclusive larval host for the Pipevine Swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor) — female butterflies seek it out specifically to lay eggs, and the caterpillars sequester the plant's defensive alkaloids for their own protection. Grow in dry, well-drained, rocky or sandy, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in a site with morning sun and afternoon shade; established plants are highly drought tolerant and should be watered only sparingly. This unassuming vine is among the most ecologically important plantings a Southwestern gardener can make for supporting native pollinators.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Transplant in spring into moist, calcareous, well-drained soil; site in partial shade with protection from hot afternoon sun, and mulch heavily to keep roots cool and moist through the first summer.

directions: Texas Dutchman's Pipe is a twining native perennial vine of moist, calcareous woodland edges and bottomland thickets in Texas and adjacent Oklahoma, recognized by its large, prominently net-veined, heart-shaped leaves and brownish-purple, pipe-shaped flowers that bloom from spring into early summer. It is a critical larval host plant for the Pipevine Swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor) in central and south Texas, and a single well-established plant can support multiple generations of caterpillars through a growing season. Grow in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline, limestone-based soil in partial shade to full sun; the plant spreads by underground rhizomes and will form a colonizing patch over time. It is an essential component of butterfly garden plantings for the Texas Hill Country, cross-timbers region, and adjacent areas with alkaline soils.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained soil with the crown set just below the soil surface; keep the root zone shaded and cool with a thick layer of mulch, and provide a trellis or fence for the twining stems to climb.

directions: White-leaf Leather Flower is a twining native perennial vine of moist woodland edges, stream banks, and thickets in the southeastern United States, instantly recognizable by the striking glaucous — blue-gray and waxy-white — undersurfaces of its leaves that flash silver whenever a breeze stirs the foliage. In spring it bears nodding, urn-shaped flowers with recurved purplish-pink to rose petals, followed by showy, feathery silvery seed heads in fall that extend the ornamental season well into winter. Grow in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun, keeping the root zone cool and consistently moist; it climbs by twining leaf petioles and benefits from a sturdy support. The glaucous foliage sets it apart from all other native clematis and makes it a distinctive choice for moist fencerows and woodland garden edges in zones 5-9.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database
